title: Dog Head Pressing Against Wall: Signs, Causes, and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your dog is pressing their head against a wall, it is a medical emergency. Learn why this happens and why you must see a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Head pressing is a life-threatening veterinary emergency indicating severe neurological issues or systemic illness. If your dog is compulsively pressing their head against hard objects, seek immediate emergency veterinary care.

What is head pressing in dogs and what does it look like?

Head pressing is a compulsive behavior where a dog stands close to a wall, corner, or other hard object and firmly presses the top of their head against it. Unlike seeking attention or a "head-butt" for affection, this behavior is usually persistent and the dog may seem stuck or distressed. It is often a sign of serious neurological impairment or damage to the forebrain.

Is head pressing in dogs a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is considered a critical medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Head pressing often indicates severe underlying issues such as brain trauma, toxic poisoning, liver failure (hepatic encephalopathy), or a brain tumor.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to resolve on their own; contact an emergency clinic right away.

How does a photo or video of my dog help with the triage process?

  • A short video or photo is incredibly valuable because neurological symptoms can be intermittent or change by the time you reach the clinic.
  • Visual evidence allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the behavior and its intensity.
  • Recordings help the vet identify accompanying signs like dilated pupils or aimless pacing, ensuring a faster and more accurate triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Behavioral changes such as abnormal postures, including "head pressing" against a wall or fence, may be observed. Head pressing, along with opisthotonos and grinding of the teeth, can be seen in certain neurological conditions.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3062)

faqs.question: What are the common causes of head pressing?
faqs.answer: Common causes include metabolic disorders like liver shunts, lead poisoning, brain tumors, strokes, or inflammatory diseases like encephalitis.

faqs.question: Is head pressing different from head rubbing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Head rubbing is often associated with skin allergies or ear infections. Head pressing involves sustained, firm pressure against a stationary object and is a neurological sign.

faqs.question: What other symptoms might I see?
faqs.answer: You may also notice aimless circling, changes in vision, seizures, or a sudden change in temperament or responsiveness.
